questions.question: Explain the Unix philosophy with respect to program design. Give an example of how this philosophy is applied.
keyPoints: Emphasizes small, modular programs that do one thing well
Programs should communicate via text streams (pipelines)
Example: combining ls, grep, wc, sort, head to achieve complex file processing without writing custom code
Each tool is reusable and can be composed with others

questions.question: Differentiate between system calls and library functions in Unix. Give two examples of each.
keyPoints: System calls are interfaces to the kernel (privileged mode)
Library functions are user-space functions that may wrap system calls
Examples of system calls: open(), read(), write(), fork(), execve()
Examples of library functions: printf(), malloc(), strcpy(), fopen()

questions.question: Describe the three main components of the Unix architecture and their functions.
keyPoints: Kernel: manages hardware resources, process scheduling, memory allocation, file system
Shell: command-line interpreter, translates user commands to system calls
File System: organizes and provides access to data on storage devices
Additional: User applications sit on top of the shell

questions.question: What is meant by 'everything is a file' in Unix? Explain its significance.
keyPoints: Devices, pipes, sockets are treated as files in Unix
Common operations (open, read, write, close) work on all file types
Simplifies I/O programming - same interface for files, keyboards, screens, networks
Enables powerful abstractions like piping and redirection

questions.question: Explain what POSIX compliance means for Unix-like operating systems.
keyPoints: POSIX is a family of standards (IEEE 1003)
Specifies API, command-line utilities, and other features
Ensures software portability across different Unix implementations
Linux, macOS, BSD are POSIX-compliant to varying degrees

questions.question: What is an inode in Unix? What information does it contain?
keyPoints: Inode is a data structure storing file metadata
Contains: file permissions, owner UID/GID, file size, timestamps, link count
Contains: pointers to data blocks on disk
Does NOT contain the filename - that's in directory entries

questions.question: Describe the process creation mechanism in Unix using fork().
keyPoints: fork() creates a new process (child) by duplicating the parent
Returns twice: once in parent (with child's PID) and once in child (with 0)
Child gets a copy of parent's address space (memory)
Parent and child execute concurrently after fork()

questions.question: Explain the Unix file permission model. How would you set permissions such that owner has full access, group can read/execute, and others have no access? Show both symbolic and octal methods.
keyPoints: File permissions are stored in 9 bits: owner (rwx), group (rwx), others (rwx)
Also includes special bits: setuid, setgid, sticky bit
Octal method: r=4, w=2, x=1; so rwx (7), r-x (5), --- (0) = 750
Symbolic method: chmod u=rwx,g=rx,o-rwx filename
Use 'chmod 750 filename' or 'chmod u=rwx,g=rx filename'
Explain how permissions affect file access and directory traversal

questions.question: Draw and explain the Unix process lifecycle. Include the states (running, ready, sleeping, zombie, stopped) and the system calls involved in process creation, execution, and termination.
keyPoints: Process states: Running (executing), Ready (waiting in queue), Sleeping (waiting for I/O), Stopped (suspended), Zombie (terminated but not reaped)
fork() creates new process, returns twice
exec() family replaces process image with new program
wait() allows parent to collect child exit status
_exit() terminates process and returns status
Zombie state occurs when process terminates but parent hasn't called wait()
Kernel reaps zombie and releases resources when parent calls wait()

questions.question: Explain the architecture of the Unix file system. Discuss the concepts of superblock, inode, data blocks, and directory entries. How are files accessed and what happens during file creation?
keyPoints: Superblock: contains file system metadata (size, free blocks, inode info)
Inode: stores file metadata and pointers to data blocks
Data blocks: actual file content stored here
Directory entries: map filenames to inode numbers
File creation: allocate free inode, create directory entry, initialize metadata
File access: path resolution traverses directories to find inode, then access data blocks
File descriptors: small integers referencing open files in per-process table
